title: -Establishing a New Schedule of Fines and Late Payment Penalties for Parking Violations; and Rescinding Resolution No. 70,924-N.S.

sections.recommendation:
Adopt a Resolution updating the existing schedule of parking violations and fines to
include parking violations and late payment penalties for California’s new daylighting
law, Assembly Bill 413 (AB 413); and rescinding resolution No. 70,924-N.S.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
The recommendation will increase revenue to the General Fund because, beginning
January 1, 2025, Parking Enforcement staff will be able to cite vehicles for parking
within an unmarked daylighting zone. However, it is not known how much revenue will
increase.
sections.current_situation:
Beginning January 1, 2025, the new state daylighting law becomes enforceable.
Implementing the law will include public education about the new law and Parking
Enforcement Officer training to implement the new law. To enforce the state law, the
City’s schedule of parking violations and fines needs to be updated with the applicable
section of the California Vehicle Code that establishes the legal framework for the
daylighting law so that officers can issue citations for vehicles illegally parked in these
zones.
The City’s support for daylighting predates the state’s action. On February 28, 2023, the
City Manager received a referral from Council titled, “Creation of an Intersection
Daylighting Policy,” to develop a comprehensive intersection daylighting policy,
including to identify priority areas for daylighting such as high-injury streets, streets and
intersections with especially high pedestrian traffic, commercial districts, and streets
near schools and colleges. Despite limited resources, Public Works has been
incorporating daylighting into the City's annual pavement rehabilitation program, Vision
Zero and transportation capital projects, traffic calming initiatives, and general work
orders. Parking Enforcement will be able to cite vehicles for violating the daylighting law

even before all daylighting zones are marked with red curbs, which will be implemented
gradually over the coming years.
sections.background:
Daylighting is a simple and effective concept that improves safety by removing parked
cars next to crosswalks to increase visibility and reduce potentially lethal collisions.
Preventing drivers from parking in the spaces directly before an intersection increases
visibility and improves sightlines for all road users, helping people see each other before
proceeding through an intersection. This low-cost measure is an extremely effective
step towards the City of Berkeley’s Vision Zero goal, as most collisions take place at or
near intersections.
AB 413, California’s new daylighting law, was signed into law in October 2023. The law
created a new section of the California Vehicle Code, section 22500(n), to prohibit
stopping, standing, or parking a vehicle within 20 feet of any unmarked or marked
crosswalk or within 15-feet of any crosswalk with a curb extension. The law went into
effect on January 1, 2024, and starting on January 1, 2025, cities can issue citations for
violating it.
ENVIRONMENTAL SUSTAINABILITY AND CLIMATE IMPACTS
Daylighting intersections improves road safety for sustainable modes of transportation,
such as walking and biking. Actions that encourage walking and biking are important
steps to reducing climate impacts and helping Berkeley achieve the goals outlined in the
City’s Climate Action Plan.
sections.rationale:
Adding a daylighting violation to the schedule of parking violations and fines would allow
the City to enforce AB 413, California’s daylighting law, to prohibit stopping, standing, or
parking a vehicle within 20 feet of any unmarked or marked crosswalk or 15 feet of any
crosswalk with a curb extension.
sections.alternatives:
No alternatives were considered. The City is subject to California state law and must be
able to enforce daylighting violations at unmarked locations. If this resolution is not
approved, then the City will not be able to enforce the state daylighting law, and people
will be able to park illegally without any consequences.
CONTACT PERSONS
Wahid Amiri, Deputy Public Works Director, Public Works, (510) 981-6396
Elliott Schwimmer, Senior Transportation Planner, Public Works, 510-981-7066

Page 3

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###N.S.
ESTABLISHING A NEW SCHEDULE FOR PARKING VIOLATIONS, FINES, AND LATE
PAYMENT PENALTIES; AND RESCINDING RESOLUTION NO.70,924-N.S.
WHEREAS, California Assembly Bill 413 amends section 22500 of the Vehicle Code to
prohibit the stopping, standing, or parking of a vehicle within 20 feet of the vehicle
approach side of any unmarked or marked crosswalk or 15 feet of any crosswalk where
a curb extension is present, starting on January 1, 2024;
WHEREAS, the City of Berkeley can begin issuing citations for violating section 22500 of
the Vehicle Code starting January 1, 2025;
WHEREAS, the Berkeley City Council has directed City staff to develop a comprehensive
intersection daylighting policy;
WHEREAS, no violation type exists in the schedule of parking violations and late payment
penalties for daylighting violations at unmarked locations;
NOW THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED by the Council of the City of Berkeley that a new
schedule of parking violations and late payment penalties is established, as set forth in
Exhibit A, which includes fines and late penalties for violations of Vehicle Code section
22500(n), pertaining to parking within 20 feet of any unmarked or marked crosswalk or
15 feet of any crosswalk where a curb extension is present.
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that Resolution No.70,924-N.S is rescinded.
Exhibits
